mechanism: Update probabilistic beliefs about a stochastic environment by observing sequential outcomes, applying Bayesian inference to compute posterior distributions over environment parameters. Use the updated beliefs to guide decision-making through utility maximization, choosing actions that maximize expected value under the current belief state. The process alternates between observation-driven belief revision and decision-making based on current beliefs, where learning processes adapt their representations to match the specific statistical structure of the task environment.

An agent samples information from a probabilistic data source according to one of two search strategies: natural sampling (observing raw instances from the population) or deliberate sampling (selecting instances targeted to specific hypotheses). After sampling, the agent performs Bayesian inference to estimate properties of the source population, computing posterior probabilities over hypotheses given the observed sample. The two strategies create systematic biases: each is unbiased for certain inferences but introduces errors for others, creating a dilemma where no single search strategy avoids all inferential mistakes.

Simulate a feature-based memory model that encodes items via feature vectors, stores them as patterns, and retrieves items through similarity-based matching and feature activation spreading. The model predicts memory accuracy and effect sizes (how much a factor changes performance) by computing retrieval probabilities from item activations. The model produces distributions of observed effect sizes, reversals in effect direction, and accuracy correlations without requiring explicit strategy assumptions or mechanisms for encoding/rehearsal changes.

Store items in episodic memory, apply a sequence of retrieval practice operations (where retrieving one item can cause forgetting of related items), and subsequently relearn forgotten items. Track how the cumulative effect of forgetting changes across multiple rounds of retrieval practice. Measure relearning benefits by comparing acquisition rate of relearned items based on their prior forgetting history. The mechanism reveals that prior forgetting of an item accelerates its subsequent relearning, demonstrating forgetting facilitates future encoding.

Present sequences of visual stimuli under two exposure conditions: intermixed (items A and B alternate on every trial) or blocked (all A trials in a contiguous block, then all B trials). Measure discrimination accuracy on a test of stimulus differentiation. The mechanism operates through associative learning where intermixed presentation activates discriminative associations between similar items through their spacing/temporal separation, enhancing learning of distinguishing features without requiring explicit spacing of presentation intervals.

Present a sequence of items to be recalled along with an irrelevant sound stream whose phonological properties may match the target items. Measure recall accuracy and error patterns under free recall versus serial recall conditions. The mechanism detects when between-sequence phonological similarity impairs retrieval by computing category-based cues: shared phonological content interferes with retrieval when category information becomes a primary retrieval aid, causing intrusions of categorically similar distractor items.

Present images paired with distractor words that are either semantically related or unrelated to the image. Vary the timing of stimulus presentation (simultaneous versus delayed distractor onset by 1000 ms) and manipulate name frequency of pictures. Measure naming latency. The mechanism detects whether interference arises from competition at the lexical selection stage or post-lexical response planning by observing when frequency effects appear and disappear relative to semantic interference effects.

Present sequences of words and nonwords to be read aloud. Track how phonological processing changes across stimulus categories by measuring effects of predictable switches between word/nonword categories. Infer changes in processing pathway (route selection) by observing when effects of one category influence the next. The mechanism assumes multiple routes for phonological encoding can be flexibly selected and that learned predictions about upcoming stimulus types cause pathway switching.

Present sequences of elements where relations can occur between adjacent elements or nonadjacent elements separated by intervening items. Vary task demands so participants must actively process certain relations while ignoring others. Measure learning of each relation type. The mechanism operates through selective attention: attended relations are learned regardless of adjacency; unattended relations are not learned regardless of proximity. Joint attentional processing of two elements is sufficient and necessary for learning their relation.

Simulate a single-system computational model of memory processes: encode items into a shared feature space, compute item activations that decay over time, use activation values to predict recognition decisions (familiarity-based) and identification latencies. The model generates predictions for hits, misses, correct rejections, and false alarms by comparing current stimulus activation to decision thresholds. Evaluate whether priming, recognition, and fluency effects emerge from a unified mechanism without invoking multiple memory systems.

Measure performance on three tasks that each test inhibitory control through different mechanisms: stop-signal reaction time (active inhibition of initiated action), inhibition of return (reactive suppression of previously attended locations), and attentional blink (reactive limitation of awareness window). Compare monolinguals versus bilinguals on each task. Infer that bilinguals show stronger effects on reactive inhibition (inhibition of return, attentional blink) but equivalent active inhibition, suggesting they have enhanced ability to maintain and bias goal-related representations.

Present word pairs (primes) whose semantic relationships are either aligned or misaligned with the structure of addition operations. On target digits following these primes, measure obligatory activation of addition facts using priming paradigm. The mechanism detects automatic analogical coordination: semantic relations in primes that structurally match arithmetic relations automatically activate corresponding addition facts, whereas primes with misaligned semantic structures do not trigger fact activation.

Present pictures (targets) with superimposed distractor pictures or words, and record eye gaze patterns and naming latencies. Manipulate semantic/phonological relatedness between target and distractor and whether the distractor item competes in naming or reading tasks. Track activation flow by measuring how gaze latencies and naming latencies change as a function of distractor relatedness. Infer that activation spreads continuously and capacity-limited from conceptual to phonological representations, with task-dependent allocation of activation.

Store a small set of items in visual working memory, then present a retro-cue that indicates which item may undergo change. After a delay, test for changes using a probe stimulus. Measure change detection accuracy. The mechanism shows that post-encoding attentional orientation to a stored item makes it resistant to interference from subsequent test probes, indicating that attended memory representations are protected from new-information interference whereas unattended items remain volatile and subject to disruption.

Present sequences of colored shapes where color-shape pairs have varying degrees of covariance. Test whether observers extract statistical regularities at the multidimensional object level (integrated color-shape combinations) or at the feature level (color and shape dimensions independently). When features covary perfectly, the learning mechanism extracts object-based regularities; when features are partially decoupled, the mechanism switches to feature-based extraction. The mechanism is sensitive to the correlation structure of multidimensional input and determines binding accordingly.

Encode a list of items, then receive an instruction to forget the list or prepare for a recall test. Vary item strength through spaced presentations (increasing context strength) or through processing time/depth (increasing item strength without context strength). Measure retention of strong versus weak items. The mechanism predicts directed forgetting depends on context-based retrieval: when strength manipulations increase shared context encoding, forgetting effectiveness increases; when strength manipulations increment item-level features, context-based forgetting remains unchanged.

Present pairs of numerals written in different notational systems (Arabic and Indian numerals). In size comparison, judge the larger numeral while ignoring notation. In same-different tasks, judge physical identity while the numerical magnitude is irrelevant. Measure whether irrelevant numerical information automatically activates despite differing notation. The mechanism assumes across-notation automatic processing: numerals in different notations are automatically mapped to an abstract magnitude representation, enabling automatic numerical comparison without deliberate conversion.

Present lists of items, then probe with positive probes (items from the list) or intrusion probes (items from the list but at a different location) or new probes. Measure recognition accuracy and rejection patterns across serial positions. Infer recognition architecture by examining whether serial position effects for intrusion probes parallel those for positive probes. The mechanism operates via dual-process theory: recognition uses familiarity (automatic) and recollection (controlled), where recollection can access items through context-to-item or item-to-context retrieval routes.

Study statements presented by two different people, then receive information that one person matches a stereotype (doctor) and the other matches a different stereotype (lawyer). On a source memory test (which person said what), measure whether items are attributed stereotype-consistently. Collect remember/know judgments to assess subjective recollection. The mechanism shows that activated stereotypes at retrieval automatically construct illusory recollections: expectations interact with memory traces to generate false recollective experiences that feel as though specific details were encoded.

Present items for storage in working memory alongside a secondary task with interleaved switches between tasks. Vary the number of task switches within a trial block while keeping item maintenance demands constant. Measure recall performance as a function of switch frequency. The mechanism detects working memory costs of task switching: switching operations impose additional processing demands on working memory resources, independent of demands from maintaining target items.
